coredata.dc:title: Composition and concentration-dependent photoluminescence of nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan.
coredata.dc:creator: Esmaeili, Mostafa
Wu, Zhiqing
Chen, Dechao
Singh, Amandeep
Sonar, Prashant
Thiel, David
Li, Qin
coredata.dc:description: Photoluminescent carbon dots have received significant research interest in recent years owing to their extraordinary optical properties, biocompatibility, and versatile functionalities. Nitrogen-doping is a widely used strategy for enhancing the photo-electronic functionalities of carbon dots. However, there is a lack of systematic study on the composition and concentration-dependency emission behaviour of N-doped carbon dots in the literature. In this study, multicolour carbon dots (CDs) having different degree of nitrogen doping were synthesized by varying the molar ratio of citric acid to urea in the precursor via hydrothermal treatment. The effects of nitrogen doping on chemical, optical, and electronic properties of CDs were characterized using various techniques including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR); fluorescence and absorption spectroscopy; fluorescence lifetime and Hall effect measurements. Three main emissive centres were recognized in concentration-dependent fluorescence study of N-CDs which can be ascribed to molecular type of fluorescence, core emission, and mid-gap nitrogen states on the edge/surface of CDs. A plausible mechanism in relation to the obtained results is proposed. This work provides insights on the opto-electro-tunability of CDs via N-doping.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nitrogen doping
Photoluminescent nanoparticles
Hall effect measurement
Photoluminescence mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-g). TEM images of the N-CDs samples A-G. The red circled area on (a) shows graphitic carbon structure. (h). Photos of sample solutions A to G from left to right under natural and UV light. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
High resolution C1s, N1s and O1s XPS spectra of samples A, D, G and graphical representation of the C, N and O compositions in all the samples (A-G).
The presence of varied functional groups displayed in FTIR spectra of the N-CDs samples A-G.

The excitation spectra of the N-CDs samples for emission wavelengths of 440, 460, 480, 500, 520 nm at concentrations of A100, B50, C50, D20, E10, F5, G3 (A), the absorption spectra of the N-CDs samples (B), the emission spectra of the N-CDs samples for excitation wavelengths of 300, 320, 340, 360, 380 nm at concentrations of A100, B50, C50, D20, E10, F5, G3 (C). Fluorescence decay curves of (A-G) N-CDs samples (D).

Peak intensity of the blue, green, yellow fluorescence centres of (A-G) N-CDs at dilutions of 0, 3, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 500 times and the graphical representation of the two curves and three centres on the excitation-emission matrix for sample B50. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Illustration of the proposed fluorescence mechanism of N-CDs at high (A), medium (B), and low (C) concentrations.
The ratio of the constituents of the prepared precursors and the elemental composition percentages of their corresponding N-CDs from the XPS survey.
Summary of fluorescence lifetimes of (A-G) N-CDs samples.
Summary of parameters from Hall effect and resistivity measurements.

head.ce:title: Composition and concentration-dependent photoluminescence of nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Queensland Micro- and Nanotechnology Centre, Griffith University, Brisbane, Queensland 4111, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Queensland Micro- and Nanotechnology Centre, Griffith University, Brisbane, Queensland 4111, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering and Built Environment, Griffith University, Nathan, Queensland 4111, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Engineering and Built Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering and Built Environment, Griffith University, Nathan, Queensland 4111, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Materials Science, School of Chemistry and Physics, Queensland University of Technology, Brisbane, Queensland 4000, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Future Environment and School of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Materials Science, School of Chemistry and Physics, Queensland University of Technology, Brisbane, Queensland 4000, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescent carbon dots have received significant research interest in recent years owing to their extraordinary optical properties, biocompatibility, and versatile functionalities. Nitrogen-doping is a widely used strategy for enhancing the photo-electronic functionalities of carbon dots. However, there is a lack of systematic study on the composition and concentration-dependency emission behaviour of N-doped carbon dots in the literature. In this study, multicolour carbon dots (CDs) having different degree of nitrogen doping were synthesized by varying the molar ratio of citric acid to urea in the precursor via hydrothermal treatment. The effects of nitrogen doping on chemical, optical, and electronic properties of CDs were characterized using various techniques including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR); fluorescence and absorption spectroscopy; fluorescence lifetime and Hall effect measurements. Three main emissive centres were recognized in concentration-dependent fluorescence study of N-CDs which can be ascribed to molecular type of fluorescence, core emission, and mid-gap nitrogen states on the edge/surface of CDs. A plausible mechanism in relation to the obtained results is proposed. This work provides insights on the opto-electro-tunability of CDs via N-doping.

ce:section.ce:para: In semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), the wavelength of fluorescence emission can be tuned by the size of nanocrystals which has significant applications in bioimaging, light emitting diodes, photovoltaic and many other electronic devices [1–3]. However, QDs generally contain heavy metals and some of these metals are rare and have limited resources. Alternatively, carbon dots (CDs) are carbogenic nanoparticles typically smaller than 50 nm. A new member of luminescent nanomaterials, CDs can offer various advantages compared to inorganic quantum dots including environment-friendliness, low cost, surface functional tuneability and scalable production [4–6]. Although size-controllable synthesis of CDs is not as straightforward as that of QDs, there are various ways to tune their chemical, optical and electronic properties namely surface functionality, heteroatom doping or even aggregation [7,8].

Heteroatom doping is a powerful approach for tailoring the intrinsic properties of CDs such as surface structure and electron distribution. Among various metallic and non-metallic heteroatoms, nitrogen is the most favourable and attractive choice of dopant for researchers of CDs. High electronegativity, size compatibility, availability of five electrons in the valence band of nitrogen in addition to facile nature of doping this element into carbon are some of the major reasons behind extensive number of reports on nitrogen doped CDs [9,10]. Nitrogen can serve as n-type impurity endowing carbon with excess electrons shifting the Fermi level which may result in modified emission of CDs [11]. Interestingly, red shift in emission of CDs due to increase of nitrogen content is observed in a few studies [12,13]. Moreover, nitrogen may not only passivate the surface of CDs causing suppression of non-radiative recombination, but also creates trap states which enhances the probability of emissive electronic transitions that finally leads to improvement of quantum yield [9].

The electronic properties of the CDs thin films are less explored in comparison to their optical properties. The generation of a voltage difference across an electrical conductor under the application of a magnetic field perpendicular to the flowing current is called Hall effect. Combined Hall and longitudinal resistance measurements can determine carrier mobility, carrier concentration, conductivity type and resistivity [14]. Though initially developed for bulk material characterisation, Hall measurement can be employed to study intrinsic electronic properties of novel nanomaterials. For instance, the occurrence of Hall effect in QDs is indicative of band-gap like charge transport similar to conventional semiconductors [15]. Nevertheless, only few studies on novel nanomaterials have tapped into Hall measurements to investigate their electronic properties [16,17]. Our recent study has found that hair-derived N-doped CDs not only form a highly effective emissive layer in the OLED, but also function as the hole-transporting material [16].

This study is aimed to provide a systematic investigation on the N-doping CDs and its effects on the optical and electronic properties. We employed a simple N-doping method of CDs by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and urea with systematically varied molar ratios. Physiochemical structures, photoluminescence and electronic properties of the resultant N-CDs were investigated. This systematic study of N-CDs sheds a ray of light on the practicality of exploiting these materials as the green substitute of traditional QDs, especially that we attempted to address the controversy over the fluorescence origin in CDs [18–20].

The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained using Kratos Axis Ultra XPS. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were gained utilizing Perkins Elmer Spectrum Two. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were taken with Hitachi HT7700 at the accelerating voltage of 120 kV. To obtain 3D fluorescence excitation-emission matrices and UV–vis absorption spectra, a Horiba Duetta fluorescence and absorbance spectrometer was used. For fluorescence lifetime measurements, the Horiba DeltaFlex time correlated single photon counting system was employed. Hall effect measurements were performed in a home-made set up under the magnetic field of 0.52 Tesla using Keithley2602B source measure meter for current application and voltage measurement.

The emission quantum yields of N-CDs were calculated using Quinine Sulphate (QS) as the reference. The UV–visible absorbance spectrum of QS and the sample solutions were recorded in 10 mm fluorescence cuvettes for at least five different concentrations with the maxima between 0 and 0.1. Exciting the same solutions with 340 nm, their fluorescence spectra were recorded, and the area under the emission curve, integrated fluorescence intensity, were measured for each of them. The integrated fluorescence intensities were plotted against the absorbance to extract slopes of interpolated lines passing the intercept of zero for each sample (Fig. S5). Numbers in the legend in Fig. S5 reflects the term Gradsam/Gradref in the following equation through which quantum yields were calculated.
Φ
s a m
=
Φ
r e f
×
Grad
sam
Grad
ref
×
η
sam
2
η
ref
2

The subscripts “sam” and “ref” stands for samples and reference, respectively. “Φ” is the emission quantum yield, Grad is the slope of the lines, η is the refractive index of the solvents. Since all the measurements for either of the reference and the samples were performed in water, the term related to refractive index does not affect this equation.

The seven vials of as-synthesized N-CDs as enlisted in Table 1 show different colours (Fig. 1.H). Their morphologies and chemical compositions were studied using transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Fourier transform infrared technique. TEM was performed to observe the shape and size of the CD samples, XPS was used to investigate the surfaces composition, and FTIR analyses were conducted to determine the presence of functional groups on the surface of the dots.

The TEM images of the seven samples (A-G) presented in Fig. 1(a-g) shows that the produced particles are all well-dispersed withing the size distribution of 2–10 nm. Sample A has the best crystallinity, as highlighted by the red circle, compared with the rest of the samples, indicating that higher urea content in the precursor mixture (CA:Urea = 2) facilitates the formation of CDs with crystalline structures. In Samples B-G, when CA:Urea ratio increases, in other words, the quantity of urea reduces in the reaction system, the CDs all appear to be amorphous without a particular trend in their morphologies. HRTEM images of the samples are provided in the Supporting Information. Although the single particles are within the range of typical dimension of CDs, they tend to assemble into aggregates in larger clusters even in very diluted solutions. Considering evidences for narrow distribution of electronic transitions in infinitely diluted CDs [7], these aggregates can be introduced as a governing parameter in photoluminescence of CDs. The solution colours of the as-synthesized CDs A-G show a somewhat consistent trend from dark to light. The black colour of N-CD A solution stands out, indicating a good yield of dispersed nanocarbons. It should be noted that the total precursor mass of N-CD A is the highest among the 7 samples.

To investigate the surface composition of the synthesized series of N-CDs, XPS analyses were conducted. The XPS survey for the samples shown in Fig. S2. contain peaks at around 283, 398, and 530 eV which correspond to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively.

The elemental composition percentages of the N-CDs from XPS survey summarized in Table 1 shows that the addition of urea not only increases the nitrogen content but also somewhat improves the carbonization. In contrast to nitrogen and carbon, the amount of oxygen increases in samples with higher CA/urea molar ratio.

Fig. 2 depicts the deconvoluted high-resolution C1s, N1s and O1s spectra of the samples A, D and G. The high-resolution spectra for the other samples can be found in supplementary information (Fig. S3). The C1s high resolution spectra contain four peaks at 284.9, 285.5, 287.5, and 289.1 eV binding energies that belong to CC, CC, CH; CO, CN; CO, CN; OCO bonds respectively. From comparing the C1s spectra, it can be again inferred that addition of urea results in higher degree of carbonization as in samples with greater amount of urea the ratio of areas under the curves of CC, CC to OCO ones tends to be generally higher indicating for existence of more carbon on the surface. The high-resolution N1s spectra comprise two peaks at 400 eV and 402 eV, respectively; the former is attributed to CN pyrrolic bonds, whilst the latter corresponds to NH/graphitic N bonds. In samples A and B, of which a higher amount of N is doped, the CN pyrrolic bonds are more prevalent than the NH/graphitic N bonds. In Sample G, which has the least amount of N, 1.93%, the percentage of CN pyrrolic bonds are less than NH/graphitic N bonds. In the high-resolution O1s spectra, two peaks at 532 and 533.5 eV attributed to CO and COC, COH, respectively, can be observed. The trend that the ratio of the area under the COC, COH curve to the CO one declines in samples with more urea suggests the presence of less OH groups on the surface and better carbonization.

Fig. 3 that show the FTIR spectra of the seven samples with IR characteristic peaks located at 785 cm−1 (NH stretch), 1174 cm−1 (CO stretch), 1398 cm−1 (CO stretch), 1557 cm−1 (CC stretch), 1655 cm−1 (CN stretch), 1700 cm−1 (CC stretch), 2982 cm−1 (CH stretch), 3284 cm−1 (OH stretch) and 3495 cm−1 (NH stretch) are observed. The presence of amine (785 cm−1), amide (1700 cm−1, 3495 cm−1), imine (1655 cm−1), alkane (2982 cm−1), carboxylic acid (1174 cm−1, 1398 cm−1, 3284 cm−1) and aromatic carbon (1557 cm−1) functional groups can be established from the FTIR spectra. From comparative evaluation of all the N-CDs spectra, a few trends were drawn out: from samples G to A (higher to lower CA/urea ratio), the peak belonging to CC gradually intensifies that is a clear evidence of higher level of carbonization; the peaks ascribed to amines and amides tend to disappear, instead, a new peak attributed to CN stretch of imines ‘carbon. Interestingly, the peaks corresponding to carboxylic acids remain unchanged for all the N-CDs.

To reveal the optical properties of the N-CDs, fluorescence emissions were mapped on 3D excitation-emission matrices at varied concentrations, ultraviolet–visible absorptions were recorded, and the efficiency of emissions were determined by quantum yield measurements.

Fig. S4 shows the 3D excitation-emission matrices of the N-CDs diluted 0, 3, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 500 times. The samples are named according to their synthesis formulation (A to G) and the degree of dilution, for example A100 refers to sample A diluted 100 times. All these measurements were performed with the very same setting of the instrument, therefore the amplitude of the curves and the colour of scale bars in these figures are reliable yardsticks for a good comparison between fluorescence emission of the samples.

On Fig. S4(A-G), two major emission curves can be distinguished in mid-range concentrations of higher carbon/nitrogen content CDs (A100, A500, B50, B100, C50, C100, D20, D50, E10). Of these two emission curves, the redder one (Curve II) is broadly excitation dependent red shifting with the increase of concentration while decreasing in the emission intensity. According to the maximum emission intensity, the optimum concentrations for Sample A-G can be determined as A100, B50, C20, D10, E5, F3, G3. The wide breadth of the Curve II is a tell-tale mark that it comprises at least two emissive centres. On the other hand, the bluer Curve (Curve I) appears in mid-range concentrations (A100, A500, B50, B100, C50, C100, D20, D50, E10), disappears at high concentrations (A50, B20, C20, D10, E5 and higher) and integrates into the Curve II at lower concentrations. Broadness of the one and only curve in B500, C500, D100, E20 indicates for the beginning of the merge of Curve I and Curve II at these concentrations. Curve I show excitation-independent emission that is a characteristic of molecular type of fluorescence. The emission peak maximum is located at 440 nm with a very narrow excitation range of 285–305 nm. Contrary to Curve II, Curve I do not shift in wavelength with the change of concentration when individually present. As an example, the two curves and three fluorescence centres are shown on the 3D excitation-emission map for sample B50 in Fig. 5.

To further illustrate the fluorescence characteristics of the samples, their emission at concentrations of A100, B50, C50, D20, E10, F5, G3 for excitation wavelengths of 300, 320, 340, 360, 380 nm are shown in a 2D plot in Fig. 4 .C. The excitation spectra of the seven samples at concentrations of A100, B50, C50, D20, E10, F5, G3 for emission wavelengths of 440, 460, 480, 500, 520 nm are also depicted on a 2D plot in Fig. 4.A. As can be seen, there are two major excitable bands around 300 nm and 360 nm for the two curves. The trend here is that in samples of more nitrogen content the two bands are distinctive, whereas with the decrease of nitrogen the 300 nm emission centre disappears accompanied with the blue shift and weakening of the band centred at 360 nm. Consistent with the XPS and FTIR data, samples A, B, C show strong similarity; Sample D starts to show the transition, and samples E-G are similar in their structures and emissive properties.
The calculated quantum yields are summarized in Table 2 . As illustrated in Fig. S5, the more nitrogen doping the higher the slope of the line, and thus the higher the quantum yield.

The UV–vis spectra of the seven samples were recorded using the same instrument setup. Concentrations of each N-CDs solutions were adjusted to have the maximum of absorption positioned approximately at 0.1. As shown in Fig. 4.B, the featureless absorption shoulder of N-CDs at 200–240 nm that is generally attributed to π*→ π transition of the sp2 carbon core bands is wider in samples with less nitrogen content. The narrower absorption shoulder in samples with higher amount of nitrogen is indicative of existence of more functional groups on the edge/surface which hinders exposition of the core to the solvent. There exists a broad band from 285 to 365 nm in the absorption spectra for all the N-CDs intensity of which enhances as the nitrogen doping increases. This band is commonly assigned to either π* → n molecular or surface/edge electronic transitions of nitrogen and oxygen containing groups, however there has been some debate on the contribution from π* → π transitions [19]. Another meaningful observation here is the greater extension of the absorption tail into the longer wavelengths in samples with higher amount of nitrogen, which is a compelling evidence showing that nitrogen states should be responsible for the reddest component of the emission spectra.

Fluorescence lifetime measurements were carried out to gain an understanding about the effect of nitrogen doping of CDs on the time electrons spend in the excited states before recombination. Hall measurements were also performed to obtain knowledge about electronic properties of N-CDs such as carrier density, resistivity, and type of majority charge transport.

To investigate the photoluminescence dynamics of N-CDs, the seven samples with the concentrations adjusted at the absorption maxima of 0.1, the same ones used for absorption spectroscopy, underwent time correlated single photon counting measurements. These measurements were performed at the excitation and emission wavelengths of 367 and 450 nm, respectively. For interpretation of the data obtained here shown in Fig. 4.D, each decay curve was fitted with a bi-exponential function. The fitted parameters are summarized in Table 3 . This table shows the average relaxation time (τav) that comprises of long (τ1) and short (τ2) decays with their corresponding contribution in percentages. The average fluorescence lifetime has the maximum value in sample D where both τ1 and τ2 are at their highest with 40.25% and 59.75% share of contribution, respectively. All the three values of delay time tend to reduce consistently from the optimum value of sample D as the nitrogen content increases or decreases in the system.

An interesting trend in this table is that from samples G to A the contribution of slow relaxation (P1) increases, and the share of fast relaxation (P2) decreases consistently. In other words, the major contribution from short decay component in samples with less amount of doped nitrogen whose core is more naked strongly suggests this fast relaxation belongs to photogenerated carriers from the sp2 domain. On the other hand, the major role of the slow relaxation in highly nitrogen doped samples whose core is buried under functional groups is attributed to recombination inside surface/edge states. The coexistence of both slow and fast decays is in agreement with the established core-surface model for intrinsic-extrinsic origin of fluorescence [21]. The results of this section provide some evidence to respond to the question either the core or edge/surface states possess the dominant role in the evolution of fluorescence in N-CDs. The short answer is that it depends on the degree of doping; in highly nitrogen doped CDs it is the edge/surface which play the more dominant role but in CDs of low nitrogen doping the carbon core may have stronger influence on the fluorescence emission.

Piranha treated square shaped cut pieces of glass with 10 mm sides were used as the substrate for this measurement. The glasses underwent sputtering for deposition of four 100 nm thick gold contacts of 1 × 1 mm on each corner. Then the freeze-dried N-CDs were doctor bladed on the fabricated substrates. To minimize the electrical contact resistance, some conductive carbon paste was also dispersed over the contact area on top of the N-CDs layer. The schematic of this structure for Hall effect and resistivity measurements are illustrated in Fig. S6. Thereafter, the sample substrates were placed under 0.52 Tesla magnetic field in our home-made Hall measurement setup, currents appropriately applied, and corresponding voltages measured with the source measure meter. The results from Hall effect and resistivity measurements are summarized in Table 4 . It is surprising to observe that in samples A and B the sign of hall voltage is negative, whereas for the rest of N-CDs this sign is positive. The negativity of the hall sign in highly nitrogen doped samples of A and B indicates that electrons are the majority carriers responsible for the electronic transport in these N-CDs. This is with the argument that nitrogen provides excess electrons for CDs. A similar phenomenon has been observed in a comparative study of pristine and nitrogen doped graphene sheet as the pristine graphene with ambipolar transport behaviour shows n-type semiconductor characteristics when doped with nitrogen [22]. The positive sign of the Hall mobility for samples G to C is likely due to the increased oxygen content in the material when less doped with nitrogen. The values of Hall mobility and majority carrier density are in order of 30–470 (cm2V-1s−1) and 1-5E11 (cm−3) respectively with no specific relation to the nitrogen content in the material. The large variability in these numbers may be due to the low conductivity and existence of varied amount of water in different samples after freeze-drying.

It appears carbon dots emission can involve various origins. The multicolour emission can be excitation-dependent, as widely reported since Y.P. Sun’s first report on CDs [23]. In the following systematic studies, evidences have been presented by a number of groups showing that CDs are all somewhat doped, often with oxygen or nitrogen, and that CDs’ fluorescence is mostly originated from core, edge and N surface states, which can emit blue, green and orange photons, respectively [24]. Recently, concentration-dependent multicolour emission has received renewed attention. Wang et al. performed time-correlated single photon counting measurements on CDs of varied concentrations [25]. The results showed that with increasing the concentration of CDs the share of fluorescence lifetime ascribed to surface states shortens at first and then prolongs; with the initial increase of the concentration more functional groups interact with each other and new energy levels appear, with further increase non-radiative relaxation are more probable to occur. The red shift was attributed to transfer of energy from smaller particles to larger ones in formation of aggregates when concentration increases.

This study also shows both elemental-doping induced multicolour emission and concentration-dependent multicolour emission. In a close inspection of the excitation and emission spectra shown in Fig. S4, three major emissive centres with blue, green, and yellow wavelengths can be identified. Fig. 5 illustrates the intensity of the three fluorescence centres for samples A-G at 0, 3, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 500 times dilutions. The omnipresence of the green centre in all sample solutions with varied degrees of nitrogen doping implies that the emission from this centre belongs to π* → π transitions of the sp2 carbon core. From the absorption results we know that nitrogen states are responsible for the reddest component of the emission herein the yellow emissive centre. The very narrow excitation range of the blue centre when not merged with the green one suggests that this emission may originate from molecular type of fluorescence (π* → n, σ* →n).

Sample C is selected as an example to study the mechanisms of the concentration-dependent emission of N-CDs. At high concentrations (C0, C3, C5, C10), the Curve II is comprised of the green and yellow emissive centres. The general trend with increasing the concentration is that the Curve II red-shifts and the emission intensity decreases. It is only in mid-range concentrations (C50, C100) that two curves both appear (Curve I the bluer one and Curve II the redder one). At low concentrations, the yellow centre disappears, and the green and blue centres merge to form Curve I. The dominance of the green centre at low concentrations is a hindrance to observe the excitation independency of the blue centre as with decreasing the concentration the green centre blue shifts from Curve II to integrate into Curve I.

The concentration-dependent multi-colour emission of N-CDs shows that there are three fluorescence centres in the system, and their prevalence is concentration-dependent. Energy transfer between the emitting centres through non-radiative cross relaxation and the existence of self-absorption/inner filter effect can describe the system. At high concentrations, there would be many and various energy states on which both radiative recombination and non-radiative cross relaxations that are probable to occur. It can be postulated that with the increase of concentration, more non-radiative cross relaxation pathways are provided for electrons to finally recombine at lower energy states. At the same time, self-absorption/inner filter effect is in place quenching the bluer region. The provision of such abundant variety of states is owing to the aggregation of CDs clusters of different sizes. The presence of these states facilitates energy transfer between the adjacent fluorescence centres, which ultimately dictates the emission characteristics. In mid-range concentrations, the reduced self-absorption/inner filter effect would allow the blue centre to appear on the excitation-emission matrices whilst the other two emissive centres have still a role to play. At low concentrations, due to the absence of aggregation, there is no cross relaxation to lower energy nitrogen states, leading to the disappearance of the yellow centre while the blue and green centres are present. Of our other observations here is that with the increase of concentration the green and yellow centres red shift along the matrices while decreasing in amplitude. Population of oxygen states which provide the pathway for cross relaxation to lower energies may explain this observation. Oxygen related groups are known to be non-emissive electron traps [26] and their existence on the surface of our N-CDs has been confirmed in the FTIR spectrum.

Our earlier research elucidated the dual role of functional groups in photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots as they contribute to both blue and red edge of the emission. It was pointed out that the bluest component shows molecular type of fluorescence characteristics [27]; it is spectrally invariable with the change of particle size or excitation energy. The concentration dependent photoluminescence study of N-CDs here also confirms that the emission of the blue centre does not change with the excitation. Furthermore, there are corroborative evidence explaining N-CDs synthesized from citric acid precursor consist of fluorescence derivatives which contribute to the blue emission [18]. Therefore, when explaining the photoluminescence mechanism, we should consider the possible role of fluorescence molecular species attached to the π-conjugated domain structure of N-CDs. These peripherally bound fluorophores may be responsible for the blue emission centre [28], especially that the purification step for our N-CDs was only filtration of the large aggregates. Our results here are consistent with the theories postulated in previous studies. Fig. 6 illustrates the competition between the three fluorescence centres at high, medium, and low concentrations in the proposed mechanism.

In summary, concentration induced multi-colour CDs were synthesized by varying the degree of nitrogen doping via the change of citric acid to urea ratio in the precursors. The effects of nitrogen doping on chemical, optical and electronic properties of CDs were systematically characterized. The absorption spectra showed that nitrogen states are responsible for the reddest component of the emission. The excitation independency of the blue component in excitation-emission matrices evidenced for existence of molecular fluorescence. Three major fluorescence centres were identified in excitation-emission matrices of the seven samples. Based on the information from the analyses, a plausible mechanism for origin of fluorescence in CDs were proposed in which the blue, green, and yellow centres were attributed to molecular fluorescence, core, and surface states emission, respectively. It is intriguing to observe that N-doping does not always result in n-type carbon dots-film. For the carbon dots synthesized in this study, there are considerable amount of oxygen element in the material, which may dominate the state of charge carriers at certain concentration ratio. The insights acquired here are instructive for designing industry-grade carbon dots, and optical and electronic devices based on them.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the funding support from the Griffith University International Postgraduate Scholarship (M.E.) and the Australian Research Council (ARC) Discovery Project DP200101105. The authors wish to thank Dr. Philip Tanner for his support in the Hall effect measurements. This work was performed in part at the Queensland node of the Australian National Fabrication Facility, a company established under the National Collaborative Research Infrastructure Strategy to provide nano- and micro-fabrication facilities for Australia's researchers. The authors also wish to thank the technical support from the Centre for Microscopy and Microanalysis at the University of Queensland.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data include sample pictures, full sets of XPS, PL data and additional illustrations. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apt.2022.103560.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: State-of-the-Art and Trends in Synthesis, Properties, and Application of Quantum Dots-Based Nanomaterials
Part. Part. Syst. Char.
